channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Three Document Blind Spots That Can Turn a Property Investment Bad
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

When investing in property, many focus solely on the purchase price, rental yields, and renovation costs. However, overlooking key legal documents can lead to unexpected complications and financial setbacks. Understanding the details within the title register, lease agreements, and search results is crucial for making informed decisions and safeguarding your investment.

The Importance of the Title Register

The title register is a fundamental document that outlines ownership details and any restrictions or covenants affecting the property. Investors may wish to scrutinise this document carefully to identify potential issues, such as shared access rights or limitations on property use. Failing to review the title register could result in unforeseen legal disputes or restrictions that hinder future plans for the property.

Lease Agreements: More Than Just Terms

For leasehold properties, the lease agreement is a critical document that dictates the rights and responsibilities of both the landlord and tenant. Investors might consider paying close attention to clauses related to ground rent, service charges, and lease extensions. A seemingly attractive property could become a financial burden if the lease terms are unfavourable or if unexpected costs arise.

Search Results: Uncovering Hidden Issues

Property searches provide valuable insights into planning permissions, environmental risks, and local infrastructure developments. Overlooking these details could lead to costly surprises, such as planning restrictions or flood risks. Borrowers may find it beneficial to thoroughly review search results to ensure the property aligns with their investment goals and avoids potential pitfalls.

By prioritising these documents alongside financial considerations, investors can make more informed decisions and mitigate risks associated with property investments. Taking the time to understand the legal framework surrounding a property could save significant time, money, and stress in the long run.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

When investing in property, many focus solely on the purchase price, rental yields, and renovation costs. However, overlooking key legal documents can lead to unexpected complications and financial setbacks. Understanding the details within the title register, lease agreements, and search results is crucial for making informed decisions and safeguarding your investment.

The Importance of the Title Register

The title register is a fundamental document that outlines ownership details and any restrictions or covenants affecting the property. Investors may wish to scrutinise this document carefully to identify potential issues, such as shared access rights or limitations on property use. Failing to review the title register could result in unforeseen legal disputes or restrictions that hinder future plans for the property.

Lease Agreements: More Than Just Terms

For leasehold properties, the lease agreement is a critical document that dictates the rights and responsibilities of both the landlord and tenant. Investors might consider paying close attention to clauses related to ground rent, service charges, and lease extensions. A seemingly attractive property could become a financial burden if the lease terms are unfavourable or if unexpected costs arise.

Search Results: Uncovering Hidden Issues

Property searches provide valuable insights into planning permissions, environmental risks, and local infrastructure developments. Overlooking these details could lead to costly surprises, such as planning restrictions or flood risks. Borrowers may find it beneficial to thoroughly review search results to ensure the property aligns with their investment goals and avoids potential pitfalls.

By prioritising these documents alongside financial considerations, investors can make more informed decisions and mitigate risks associated with property investments. Taking the time to understand the legal framework surrounding a property could save significant time, money, and stress in the long run.

item.title: 7 Ways to Lower Your Mortgage Costs in 2026
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Seven Potential Ways to Reduce Mortgage Costs in 2026

With rising interest rates continuing to impact homeowners across the UK, many borrowers may be seeking ways to manage their mortgage payments more effectively. Those coming to the end of fixed-rate deals could find themselves facing significantly higher repayments, making it worthwhile to explore options that might help reduce costs.

Reviewing Your Mortgage Options

Homeowners approaching the end of their current mortgage term might consider:

  • Comparing remortgage deals well in advance of their fixed rate ending
  • Exploring whether switching to a different mortgage type could prove beneficial
  • Checking eligibility for lower-rate products based on current loan-to-value ratios

Alternative Approaches to Reduce Payments

Beyond simply remortgaging, borrowers might want to investigate:

  • The potential benefits of extending mortgage terms to lower monthly payments
  • Whether overpayments during lower-rate periods could reduce overall interest
  • The feasibility of offset mortgages for those with significant savings
  • Government support schemes that may be available in 2026

With mortgage costs remaining a significant financial consideration, taking time to research options and seek professional advice could help homeowners make informed decisions about managing their payments in the coming year.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Seven Potential Ways to Reduce Mortgage Costs in 2026

With rising interest rates continuing to impact homeowners across the UK, many borrowers may be seeking ways to manage their mortgage payments more effectively. Those coming to the end of fixed-rate deals could find themselves facing significantly higher repayments, making it worthwhile to explore options that might help reduce costs.

Reviewing Your Mortgage Options

Homeowners approaching the end of their current mortgage term might consider:

  • Comparing remortgage deals well in advance of their fixed rate ending
  • Exploring whether switching to a different mortgage type could prove beneficial
  • Checking eligibility for lower-rate products based on current loan-to-value ratios

Alternative Approaches to Reduce Payments

Beyond simply remortgaging, borrowers might want to investigate:

  • The potential benefits of extending mortgage terms to lower monthly payments
  • Whether overpayments during lower-rate periods could reduce overall interest
  • The feasibility of offset mortgages for those with significant savings
  • Government support schemes that may be available in 2026

With mortgage costs remaining a significant financial consideration, taking time to research options and seek professional advice could help homeowners make informed decisions about managing their payments in the coming year.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #439
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rental affordability crisis fuels surge in guarantor demand

The introduction of the Renters Reform Act has led to a sharp increase in tenants requiring guarantors, with new data suggesting one in two renters may now struggle to pass affordability checks. With average monthly rents reaching £1,438, tenants would typically need an annual income of £43,140 to secure a property - significantly above the UK average wage of £41,859.

Analysis reveals nearly 20% of tenants across 288 local authorities may need guarantors, particularly affecting:

  • Younger renters
  • Overseas applicants
  • Self-employed workers
  • Those moving to high-cost rental areas

Industry experts suggest this trend may continue as landlords tighten affordability criteria following the ban on large rent-in-advance payments. Sam Reynolds of Zero Deposits commented: "Guarantors are becoming an essential requirement for many tenants who fall outside traditional affordability measures."

Compliance concerns emerge under new rental reforms

A recent survey highlights growing tenant scepticism about landlord compliance with the Renters Reform Act. The research found:

  • Only 32% of tenants believe their landlord is fully compliant
  • 42% report some compliance standards aren't being met
  • 26% are unaware whether their landlord meets the new requirements

Despite the legislation, 91% of tenants surveyed noticed no significant improvements in their rental conditions. Property professionals warn this represents a critical moment for the sector. Roma Sharma of Rushbrook & Rathbone noted: "Landlords and agents must demonstrate transparent communication and decisive action to meet both legal obligations and tenant expectations."

Social housing sector faces new overheating regulations

The government is expanding Awaab's Law to address excessive heat in social housing, following research showing 10.6% of tenants report uncomfortably hot living conditions. The new provisions, coming into force this month, will require social landlords to:

  • Implement clearer assessment methods for thermal hazards
  • Respond promptly to overheating complaints
  • Maintain properties within safe temperature ranges year-round

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook emphasised these changes will help ensure social homes remain habitable in all weather conditions. The reforms follow several high-profile cases where tenants claimed their properties became uninhabitable during heatwaves, with some indoor temperatures reportedly reaching 43°C.

As the rental market continues to evolve, both private and social landlords face growing challenges in meeting changing regulations while maintaining viable tenancies. With affordability constraints tightening and compliance standards rising, the sector may need to adapt its practices to accommodate these shifting demands.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rental affordability crisis fuels surge in guarantor demand

The introduction of the Renters Reform Act has led to a sharp increase in tenants requiring guarantors, with new data suggesting one in two renters may now struggle to pass affordability checks. With average monthly rents reaching £1,438, tenants would typically need an annual income of £43,140 to secure a property - significantly above the UK average wage of £41,859.

Analysis reveals nearly 20% of tenants across 288 local authorities may need guarantors, particularly affecting:

  • Younger renters
  • Overseas applicants
  • Self-employed workers
  • Those moving to high-cost rental areas

Industry experts suggest this trend may continue as landlords tighten affordability criteria following the ban on large rent-in-advance payments. Sam Reynolds of Zero Deposits commented: "Guarantors are becoming an essential requirement for many tenants who fall outside traditional affordability measures."

Compliance concerns emerge under new rental reforms

A recent survey highlights growing tenant scepticism about landlord compliance with the Renters Reform Act. The research found:

  • Only 32% of tenants believe their landlord is fully compliant
  • 42% report some compliance standards aren't being met
  • 26% are unaware whether their landlord meets the new requirements

Despite the legislation, 91% of tenants surveyed noticed no significant improvements in their rental conditions. Property professionals warn this represents a critical moment for the sector. Roma Sharma of Rushbrook & Rathbone noted: "Landlords and agents must demonstrate transparent communication and decisive action to meet both legal obligations and tenant expectations."

Social housing sector faces new overheating regulations

The government is expanding Awaab's Law to address excessive heat in social housing, following research showing 10.6% of tenants report uncomfortably hot living conditions. The new provisions, coming into force this month, will require social landlords to:

  • Implement clearer assessment methods for thermal hazards
  • Respond promptly to overheating complaints
  • Maintain properties within safe temperature ranges year-round

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook emphasised these changes will help ensure social homes remain habitable in all weather conditions. The reforms follow several high-profile cases where tenants claimed their properties became uninhabitable during heatwaves, with some indoor temperatures reportedly reaching 43°C.

As the rental market continues to evolve, both private and social landlords face growing challenges in meeting changing regulations while maintaining viable tenancies. With affordability constraints tightening and compliance standards rising, the sector may need to adapt its practices to accommodate these shifting demands.

item.title: Third-party reliance leaves many estate agents without direct website control
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research reveals that a majority of UK estate agents lack direct control over their own websites, with many relying heavily on external providers for basic updates. The findings highlight ongoing challenges in the property sector's digital infrastructure, with cost concerns and operational disruption frequently delaying much-needed technology upgrades.

Third-party dependence remains widespread

A survey of 234 estate agents found that nearly 60% depend on third-party providers for website management. The breakdown shows:

  • 31% completely reliant on external developers
  • 28% fairly reliant on suppliers
  • 12% fully self-sufficient in managing their digital presence

This reliance on external support creates several operational challenges for agencies. Many respondents reported experiencing slow turnaround times for updates, while others highlighted the significant costs associated with making even minor website changes through third parties.

Technology upgrade delays common

The research also examined attitudes towards system upgrades, finding that 63% of estate agents had postponed technology improvements. The main reasons cited include:

  • 34% concerned about operational disruption
  • 31% worried about migration and implementation costs

Industry experts suggest this reluctance to upgrade may leave many agencies working with outdated systems that are difficult to modify and expensive to maintain. Heather Staff of Street Group commented: "For too long, estate agencies have been forced to rely on third-party providers for even the most basic changes to their digital infrastructure."

The findings provide valuable insights into the digital challenges facing estate agents, suggesting many might benefit from reviewing their current website management solutions. As the property market becomes increasingly digital, having greater control over online presence could prove crucial for agencies looking to remain competitive.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research reveals that a majority of UK estate agents lack direct control over their own websites, with many relying heavily on external providers for basic updates. The findings highlight ongoing challenges in the property sector's digital infrastructure, with cost concerns and operational disruption frequently delaying much-needed technology upgrades.

Third-party dependence remains widespread

A survey of 234 estate agents found that nearly 60% depend on third-party providers for website management. The breakdown shows:

  • 31% completely reliant on external developers
  • 28% fairly reliant on suppliers
  • 12% fully self-sufficient in managing their digital presence

This reliance on external support creates several operational challenges for agencies. Many respondents reported experiencing slow turnaround times for updates, while others highlighted the significant costs associated with making even minor website changes through third parties.

Technology upgrade delays common

The research also examined attitudes towards system upgrades, finding that 63% of estate agents had postponed technology improvements. The main reasons cited include:

  • 34% concerned about operational disruption
  • 31% worried about migration and implementation costs

Industry experts suggest this reluctance to upgrade may leave many agencies working with outdated systems that are difficult to modify and expensive to maintain. Heather Staff of Street Group commented: "For too long, estate agencies have been forced to rely on third-party providers for even the most basic changes to their digital infrastructure."

The findings provide valuable insights into the digital challenges facing estate agents, suggesting many might benefit from reviewing their current website management solutions. As the property market becomes increasingly digital, having greater control over online presence could prove crucial for agencies looking to remain competitive.

item.title: Zoopla launches masterclass video series to help agents convert more leads
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Zoopla has introduced a new educational resource for estate agents looking to enhance their lead conversion strategies. The property portal's Masterclass video series offers practical guidance on turning valuation opportunities into confirmed instructions, with the content being made available to agents through Zoopla's professional platforms.

Masterclass Series for Estate Agents

The six-part training programme, available at no additional cost to ZooplaPro subscribers, focuses on improving agents' ability to secure vendor instructions. The first episode, titled 'Win More Leads: Become a Conversion Master', is also accessible to non-subscribers via Zoopla for Business.

Presented by industry expert Ian Preston, the inaugural session addresses several key areas:

  • Common reasons for losing potential listings
  • Tailoring valuation presentations to different seller profiles
  • Effective follow-up techniques after property valuations
  • Communication strategies for arranging and conducting valuation appointments

Upcoming Content and Accessibility

A second instalment, 'Flexible Fees to the Revenue Rescue', is scheduled for release in June. This forthcoming module will explore alternative fee arrangements for estate agents, including approaches for presenting these options to sellers and handling potential concerns.

While the complete Masterclass series remains exclusive to ZooplaPro members, the property portal has made selected content available to the wider agency community. Non-subscribers might consider accessing the introductory episode and accompanying podcast material through Zoopla for Business to evaluate the programme's potential value.

Rich Hayes, Zoopla's Chief Operating Officer, commented: "Lead conversion remains fundamental to agency success. Through this Masterclass initiative, we're providing our partner agents with expert-led strategies to enhance their instruction rates and business performance."

For estate agents seeking to refine their conversion techniques, Zoopla's Masterclass series may offer valuable insights from industry specialists. The staggered release schedule allows participants to implement strategies between sessions, whilst the tiered access model provides options for both Zoopla customers and non-subscribers.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Zoopla has introduced a new educational resource for estate agents looking to enhance their lead conversion strategies. The property portal's Masterclass video series offers practical guidance on turning valuation opportunities into confirmed instructions, with the content being made available to agents through Zoopla's professional platforms.

Masterclass Series for Estate Agents

The six-part training programme, available at no additional cost to ZooplaPro subscribers, focuses on improving agents' ability to secure vendor instructions. The first episode, titled 'Win More Leads: Become a Conversion Master', is also accessible to non-subscribers via Zoopla for Business.

Presented by industry expert Ian Preston, the inaugural session addresses several key areas:

  • Common reasons for losing potential listings
  • Tailoring valuation presentations to different seller profiles
  • Effective follow-up techniques after property valuations
  • Communication strategies for arranging and conducting valuation appointments

Upcoming Content and Accessibility

A second instalment, 'Flexible Fees to the Revenue Rescue', is scheduled for release in June. This forthcoming module will explore alternative fee arrangements for estate agents, including approaches for presenting these options to sellers and handling potential concerns.

While the complete Masterclass series remains exclusive to ZooplaPro members, the property portal has made selected content available to the wider agency community. Non-subscribers might consider accessing the introductory episode and accompanying podcast material through Zoopla for Business to evaluate the programme's potential value.

Rich Hayes, Zoopla's Chief Operating Officer, commented: "Lead conversion remains fundamental to agency success. Through this Masterclass initiative, we're providing our partner agents with expert-led strategies to enhance their instruction rates and business performance."

For estate agents seeking to refine their conversion techniques, Zoopla's Masterclass series may offer valuable insights from industry specialists. The staggered release schedule allows participants to implement strategies between sessions, whilst the tiered access model provides options for both Zoopla customers and non-subscribers.

item.title: Leasehold campaigners blast anonymous group over reform challenge ‘to protect profits’
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The National Leasehold Campaign has strongly criticised reports of an anonymous group considering legal action against the government’s leasehold reforms. The campaign group alleges that unnamed interests are attempting to obstruct changes designed to benefit homeowners, describing the move as "lawfare" aimed at delaying or derailing crucial reforms. This development comes at a critical time, as the government seeks to overhaul the leasehold system, which has long been criticised for its unfair practices.

Anonymous Group Threatens Legal Action

Recent reports suggest that an unnamed organisation is exploring legal avenues to challenge elements of the government’s leasehold reform programme. The National Leasehold Campaign has raised concerns that this represents a coordinated effort by vested interests to protect their profits and delay justice for millions of leaseholders. Katie Kendrick, founder of the campaign, has condemned the move, stating that it is a predictable yet astonishing attempt to stall long-overdue reform.

Kendrick emphasised that these anonymous groups often emerge at the eleventh hour, presenting themselves as advocates for property rights or freeholders but refusing to disclose their funding or leadership. She argued that such tactics undermine democracy and serve only to protect the interests of those benefiting from the current, flawed system.

A Decade of Advocacy for Leasehold Reform

The National Leasehold Campaign has spent nearly a decade advocating for reform, highlighting issues such as spiralling costs, unfair ground rents, and the lack of control leaseholders have over their own homes. The campaign argues that the leasehold system is outdated and feudal, trapping millions of homeowners in an unfair framework. Despite years of grassroots efforts to bring about change, those now threatening legal action remained silent, content to profit from the broken status quo.

Kendrick pointed out that the sudden emergence of these anonymous groups coincides with the government’s push for reform, suggesting that their motives are driven by self-interest rather than genuine concern for property rights. She urged transparency, calling on these groups to openly stand behind their positions rather than hiding behind anonymity.

The Battle for Fairness Continues

The National Leasehold Campaign remains steadfast in its commitment to achieving fair and meaningful reform. The group’s efforts have been instrumental in exposing the flaws of the leasehold system and pushing for change. As the government moves forward with its reform agenda, the campaign warns against letting anonymous interests derail progress.

Leaseholders across the UK have long awaited reform, and the National Leasehold Campaign continues to advocate for their rights. With the threat of legal action looming, the battle for fairness in the leasehold system remains as crucial as ever.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The National Leasehold Campaign has strongly criticised reports of an anonymous group considering legal action against the government’s leasehold reforms. The campaign group alleges that unnamed interests are attempting to obstruct changes designed to benefit homeowners, describing the move as "lawfare" aimed at delaying or derailing crucial reforms. This development comes at a critical time, as the government seeks to overhaul the leasehold system, which has long been criticised for its unfair practices.

Anonymous Group Threatens Legal Action

Recent reports suggest that an unnamed organisation is exploring legal avenues to challenge elements of the government’s leasehold reform programme. The National Leasehold Campaign has raised concerns that this represents a coordinated effort by vested interests to protect their profits and delay justice for millions of leaseholders. Katie Kendrick, founder of the campaign, has condemned the move, stating that it is a predictable yet astonishing attempt to stall long-overdue reform.

Kendrick emphasised that these anonymous groups often emerge at the eleventh hour, presenting themselves as advocates for property rights or freeholders but refusing to disclose their funding or leadership. She argued that such tactics undermine democracy and serve only to protect the interests of those benefiting from the current, flawed system.

A Decade of Advocacy for Leasehold Reform

The National Leasehold Campaign has spent nearly a decade advocating for reform, highlighting issues such as spiralling costs, unfair ground rents, and the lack of control leaseholders have over their own homes. The campaign argues that the leasehold system is outdated and feudal, trapping millions of homeowners in an unfair framework. Despite years of grassroots efforts to bring about change, those now threatening legal action remained silent, content to profit from the broken status quo.

Kendrick pointed out that the sudden emergence of these anonymous groups coincides with the government’s push for reform, suggesting that their motives are driven by self-interest rather than genuine concern for property rights. She urged transparency, calling on these groups to openly stand behind their positions rather than hiding behind anonymity.

The Battle for Fairness Continues

The National Leasehold Campaign remains steadfast in its commitment to achieving fair and meaningful reform. The group’s efforts have been instrumental in exposing the flaws of the leasehold system and pushing for change. As the government moves forward with its reform agenda, the campaign warns against letting anonymous interests derail progress.

Leaseholders across the UK have long awaited reform, and the National Leasehold Campaign continues to advocate for their rights. With the threat of legal action looming, the battle for fairness in the leasehold system remains as crucial as ever.

item.title: UK property market: What’s really happening right now?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market showed signs of seasonal softening during the late May bank holiday week, with most activity metrics dipping around 20% compared to normal working weeks. Despite this expected slowdown, underlying market conditions remained stable, with no dramatic shifts in the broader trends shaping the housing landscape.

Listings and pricing trends

New property listings fell to 31,300 during Week 21 (ending 31 May 2026), down from 39,600 the previous week and below the 2026 weekly average of 37,600. However, year-to-date figures reveal the market remains more active than previous years, with 783,000 new listings so far in 2026 – 0.6% ahead of 2025 and 14.1% above the 2017–19 average.

Price reductions affected 21,000 properties this week, representing 13.4% of all UK homes on the market in May. This continues the recent trend of higher reduction rates compared to historical norms, with the 2025 average at 12.8% versus a six-year average of 10.7%.

Sales activity and market dynamics

Gross residential sales agreed dipped to 22,100 during the bank holiday week, down from 27,200 the previous week. The year-to-date total stands at 521,000 properties sold subject to contract – 5.6% lower than 2025 but 9.6% above pre-pandemic levels.

Notably, the gap between initial asking prices and sale-agreed asking prices has widened to 21.5%, significantly above the 10-year average of 16–17%. This suggests buyers are negotiating harder, with the average sale-agreed price coming in at £368,000 compared to £447,000 for listings.

The sell-through rate (homes going SSTC as a proportion of agents' stock) stood at 14.6% in May, slightly below the pre-Covid average of 15.5%. Meanwhile, the sale fall-through rate improved to 21.5%, better than the decade average of 24.5%.

Market outlook

While seasonal factors temporarily dampened activity, the market continues to show resilience compared to pre-pandemic levels. Homeowners considering selling might note the current probability of successful completion stands at 53.9% for properties leaving agents' books, though this figure may adjust as more May data becomes available.

With price negotiations becoming more significant and reduction rates elevated, buyers appear to be exercising greater caution. However, the improved fall-through rate suggests transactions that do proceed may be on more stable footing than in previous years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market showed signs of seasonal softening during the late May bank holiday week, with most activity metrics dipping around 20% compared to normal working weeks. Despite this expected slowdown, underlying market conditions remained stable, with no dramatic shifts in the broader trends shaping the housing landscape.

Listings and pricing trends

New property listings fell to 31,300 during Week 21 (ending 31 May 2026), down from 39,600 the previous week and below the 2026 weekly average of 37,600. However, year-to-date figures reveal the market remains more active than previous years, with 783,000 new listings so far in 2026 – 0.6% ahead of 2025 and 14.1% above the 2017–19 average.

Price reductions affected 21,000 properties this week, representing 13.4% of all UK homes on the market in May. This continues the recent trend of higher reduction rates compared to historical norms, with the 2025 average at 12.8% versus a six-year average of 10.7%.

Sales activity and market dynamics

Gross residential sales agreed dipped to 22,100 during the bank holiday week, down from 27,200 the previous week. The year-to-date total stands at 521,000 properties sold subject to contract – 5.6% lower than 2025 but 9.6% above pre-pandemic levels.

Notably, the gap between initial asking prices and sale-agreed asking prices has widened to 21.5%, significantly above the 10-year average of 16–17%. This suggests buyers are negotiating harder, with the average sale-agreed price coming in at £368,000 compared to £447,000 for listings.

The sell-through rate (homes going SSTC as a proportion of agents' stock) stood at 14.6% in May, slightly below the pre-Covid average of 15.5%. Meanwhile, the sale fall-through rate improved to 21.5%, better than the decade average of 24.5%.

Market outlook

While seasonal factors temporarily dampened activity, the market continues to show resilience compared to pre-pandemic levels. Homeowners considering selling might note the current probability of successful completion stands at 53.9% for properties leaving agents' books, though this figure may adjust as more May data becomes available.

With price negotiations becoming more significant and reduction rates elevated, buyers appear to be exercising greater caution. However, the improved fall-through rate suggests transactions that do proceed may be on more stable footing than in previous years.

item.title: Most tenants unconvinced agents are compliant with Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Tenants Express Doubts Over Property Managers' Compliance With Renters' Rights Act

New research suggests many tenants remain unconvinced that property managers have fully adapted to the requirements of the Renters' Rights Act. A survey of 1,105 tenants in managed properties found only 32% believe their property manager is fully compliant with the legislation, whilst 42% suspect gaps in compliance. Over a quarter (26%) admitted they simply don't know whether their manager meets the new standards.

Communication Gaps and Documentation Concerns

The findings highlight significant communication challenges between property managers and tenants:

  • 59% of tenants reported not receiving updated tenancy agreements reflecting the Act's changes
  • 50% said they'd received no information from their property manager about the reforms
  • Only 29% felt the changes had been clearly explained to them

With the 31 May deadline for issuing revised documentation now passed, these figures suggest many managing agents may need to review their communication strategies.

Tenants Report Limited Visible Changes

The survey revealed concerns about whether the legislation is translating into tangible improvements:

  • 91% of tenants noticed no significant change in how their property is managed since the Act came into force
  • Only 19% said their manager had proactively addressed issues like damp, mould or safety concerns

Property industry experts suggest these findings indicate some managers may be treating compliance as a box-ticking exercise rather than an opportunity to genuinely improve standards. Tenants looking for reassurance about their rights might consider reviewing the government's guidance on the Renters' Rights Act or contacting their local Citizens Advice bureau for support.

As the property sector adapts to these legislative changes, both tenants and responsible operators may find value in maintaining open communication about rights, responsibilities and property maintenance standards.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Tenants Express Doubts Over Property Managers' Compliance With Renters' Rights Act

New research suggests many tenants remain unconvinced that property managers have fully adapted to the requirements of the Renters' Rights Act. A survey of 1,105 tenants in managed properties found only 32% believe their property manager is fully compliant with the legislation, whilst 42% suspect gaps in compliance. Over a quarter (26%) admitted they simply don't know whether their manager meets the new standards.

Communication Gaps and Documentation Concerns

The findings highlight significant communication challenges between property managers and tenants:

  • 59% of tenants reported not receiving updated tenancy agreements reflecting the Act's changes
  • 50% said they'd received no information from their property manager about the reforms
  • Only 29% felt the changes had been clearly explained to them

With the 31 May deadline for issuing revised documentation now passed, these figures suggest many managing agents may need to review their communication strategies.

Tenants Report Limited Visible Changes

The survey revealed concerns about whether the legislation is translating into tangible improvements:

  • 91% of tenants noticed no significant change in how their property is managed since the Act came into force
  • Only 19% said their manager had proactively addressed issues like damp, mould or safety concerns

Property industry experts suggest these findings indicate some managers may be treating compliance as a box-ticking exercise rather than an opportunity to genuinely improve standards. Tenants looking for reassurance about their rights might consider reviewing the government's guidance on the Renters' Rights Act or contacting their local Citizens Advice bureau for support.

As the property sector adapts to these legislative changes, both tenants and responsible operators may find value in maintaining open communication about rights, responsibilities and property maintenance standards.

item.title: Landlord possession claims increased ahead of Renters’ Rights Act changes
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlord Possession Claims Rise Ahead of Renters' Rights Act

New data reveals a notable increase in landlord possession claims during the first quarter of 2026, as property owners appeared to accelerate legal action before the introduction of the Renters' Rights Act. Analysis by LegalforLandlords shows a 5.9% quarterly rise in possession claims, reaching 22,733 cases between January and March. Private landlords drove much of this increase, with their claims jumping by 11.1% compared to the previous quarter.

Changing Landscape for Landlords

The figures follow a period of declining possession activity throughout 2025, with accelerated claims having fallen by over 10% in the final quarter before rebounding sharply in early 2026. Industry experts suggest this pattern reflects landlords' responses to impending legislative changes:

  • Private landlords accounted for the majority of increased claims
  • Social landlord claims rose by a more modest 5%
  • Accelerated possession claims across all sectors increased by 1.7%

The Renters' Rights Act, which came into force on 1 May 2026, abolished Section 21 'no-fault' evictions and introduced a reformed possession process. LegalforLandlords' analysis indicates many landlords chose to act while previous regulations remained in place.

Sector Concerns About Future Impacts

Sim Sekhon, Group CEO at LegalforLandlords, commented: "The timing of these increases is highly significant. Many landlords clearly decided to act before the legislation changed the possession process permanently." The organisation noted ongoing concerns within the property sector about how the reforms might affect:

  • Landlords' ability to manage rental risks
  • Processes for dealing with tenant arrears
  • Future investment in private rental housing

With rental supply already under pressure in many areas, some industry voices have warned that the changes could further discourage landlord participation in the market. However, the full impact of the Renters' Rights Act on possession trends may only become clear in subsequent quarters.

As the rental market adapts to these legislative changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the new possession framework. Professional advice could prove valuable for those navigating the evolving rental landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlord Possession Claims Rise Ahead of Renters' Rights Act

New data reveals a notable increase in landlord possession claims during the first quarter of 2026, as property owners appeared to accelerate legal action before the introduction of the Renters' Rights Act. Analysis by LegalforLandlords shows a 5.9% quarterly rise in possession claims, reaching 22,733 cases between January and March. Private landlords drove much of this increase, with their claims jumping by 11.1% compared to the previous quarter.

Changing Landscape for Landlords

The figures follow a period of declining possession activity throughout 2025, with accelerated claims having fallen by over 10% in the final quarter before rebounding sharply in early 2026. Industry experts suggest this pattern reflects landlords' responses to impending legislative changes:

  • Private landlords accounted for the majority of increased claims
  • Social landlord claims rose by a more modest 5%
  • Accelerated possession claims across all sectors increased by 1.7%

The Renters' Rights Act, which came into force on 1 May 2026, abolished Section 21 'no-fault' evictions and introduced a reformed possession process. LegalforLandlords' analysis indicates many landlords chose to act while previous regulations remained in place.

Sector Concerns About Future Impacts

Sim Sekhon, Group CEO at LegalforLandlords, commented: "The timing of these increases is highly significant. Many landlords clearly decided to act before the legislation changed the possession process permanently." The organisation noted ongoing concerns within the property sector about how the reforms might affect:

  • Landlords' ability to manage rental risks
  • Processes for dealing with tenant arrears
  • Future investment in private rental housing

With rental supply already under pressure in many areas, some industry voices have warned that the changes could further discourage landlord participation in the market. However, the full impact of the Renters' Rights Act on possession trends may only become clear in subsequent quarters.

As the rental market adapts to these legislative changes, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the new possession framework. Professional advice could prove valuable for those navigating the evolving rental landscape.

item.title: Lomond data points to sustained upward pressure on rents
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Sustained Growth Amid Strong Demand

New data from Lomond’s Quarterly Insights Report reveals continued upward pressure on rental prices across the UK, with average rents rising from £1,245 to £1,384 per calendar month in Q1 2026 compared to the same period last year. The findings highlight widespread rental growth across all monitored regions, with London maintaining its position as the most expensive market at £2,339pcm – 69% above the national average.

Kent recorded the sharpest annual increase at 9%, pushing average rents to £1,031pcm. Other regions saw more moderate but consistent growth, including Yorkshire (5%), the North West (4%), and both the Midlands and Scotland (3%). The South of England experienced a 2% rise, with rents reaching £1,309pcm.

Tenant Demand Remains Resilient

Market activity appears robust ahead of the Renters’ Rights Act implementation on 1 May, with tenant viewings increasing by 28% and letting appraisals jumping 62% quarter-on-quarter. This suggests sustained demand for rental properties despite ongoing regulatory changes affecting the private rented sector.

Ed Phillips, Lomond’s Group Chief Executive, commented: “The rental market has maintained strong momentum this year, with price growth evident across both urban and rural locations. Rising viewings and appraisal activity indicate landlords are actively assessing opportunities in what remains a competitive environment.”

Phillips added: “Moving forward, the interplay between supply and demand will continue shaping market dynamics. Landlords may wish to pay close attention to local conditions, as regional variations in growth rates could influence investment decisions.”

With rental demand outpacing supply in many areas and affordability constraints keeping potential buyers in the rental market longer, these trends may persist throughout 2026. Prospective tenants might consider reviewing their budgets carefully, whilst landlords could benefit from monitoring local market movements closely.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Sustained Growth Amid Strong Demand

New data from Lomond’s Quarterly Insights Report reveals continued upward pressure on rental prices across the UK, with average rents rising from £1,245 to £1,384 per calendar month in Q1 2026 compared to the same period last year. The findings highlight widespread rental growth across all monitored regions, with London maintaining its position as the most expensive market at £2,339pcm – 69% above the national average.

Kent recorded the sharpest annual increase at 9%, pushing average rents to £1,031pcm. Other regions saw more moderate but consistent growth, including Yorkshire (5%), the North West (4%), and both the Midlands and Scotland (3%). The South of England experienced a 2% rise, with rents reaching £1,309pcm.

Tenant Demand Remains Resilient

Market activity appears robust ahead of the Renters’ Rights Act implementation on 1 May, with tenant viewings increasing by 28% and letting appraisals jumping 62% quarter-on-quarter. This suggests sustained demand for rental properties despite ongoing regulatory changes affecting the private rented sector.

Ed Phillips, Lomond’s Group Chief Executive, commented: “The rental market has maintained strong momentum this year, with price growth evident across both urban and rural locations. Rising viewings and appraisal activity indicate landlords are actively assessing opportunities in what remains a competitive environment.”

Phillips added: “Moving forward, the interplay between supply and demand will continue shaping market dynamics. Landlords may wish to pay close attention to local conditions, as regional variations in growth rates could influence investment decisions.”

With rental demand outpacing supply in many areas and affordability constraints keeping potential buyers in the rental market longer, these trends may persist throughout 2026. Prospective tenants might consider reviewing their budgets carefully, whilst landlords could benefit from monitoring local market movements closely.

item.title: Selling a House With a Short Lease: What Scottish Flat Owners Need to Know
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Whilst leasehold properties dominate England’s flat market, Scotland’s property landscape operates quite differently. Most Scottish flats are either held under common ownership governed by the Tenements (Scotland) Act 2004 or come with lengthy leases that rarely cause issues during sales. However, homeowners with shorter leases might face unique challenges when selling their property.

Understanding Scotland’s Leasehold Landscape

Unlike in England and Wales, leasehold arrangements in Scotland are relatively uncommon for residential properties. The majority of flats fall under one of two categories:

  • Common ownership through the Tenements (Scotland) Act 2004
  • Long leases (typically 100+ years) that don’t typically affect property transactions

This system generally makes buying, selling and mortgaging Scottish flats more straightforward than in other parts of the UK. However, properties with shorter leases – particularly those under 70 years – may encounter difficulties during the sales process.

Challenges of Selling With a Short Lease

Flats with diminishing leases can present several hurdles for Scottish homeowners looking to sell:

  • Mortgage lenders often hesitate to approve loans for properties with leases under 70 years
  • Potential buyers might be put off by the prospect of lease extension costs
  • The property’s value could be significantly lower than comparable homes with longer leases

Homeowners in this position might wish to explore options such as negotiating a lease extension before putting their property on the market. In some cases, it could be worth consulting a solicitor specialising in Scottish property law to understand all available options.

Whilst short leases are less common in Scotland’s property market, being aware of potential complications could help homeowners navigate the sales process more effectively. Those concerned about their lease length might consider seeking professional advice early to understand how it could affect their selling prospects.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Whilst leasehold properties dominate England’s flat market, Scotland’s property landscape operates quite differently. Most Scottish flats are either held under common ownership governed by the Tenements (Scotland) Act 2004 or come with lengthy leases that rarely cause issues during sales. However, homeowners with shorter leases might face unique challenges when selling their property.

Understanding Scotland’s Leasehold Landscape

Unlike in England and Wales, leasehold arrangements in Scotland are relatively uncommon for residential properties. The majority of flats fall under one of two categories:

  • Common ownership through the Tenements (Scotland) Act 2004
  • Long leases (typically 100+ years) that don’t typically affect property transactions

This system generally makes buying, selling and mortgaging Scottish flats more straightforward than in other parts of the UK. However, properties with shorter leases – particularly those under 70 years – may encounter difficulties during the sales process.

Challenges of Selling With a Short Lease

Flats with diminishing leases can present several hurdles for Scottish homeowners looking to sell:

  • Mortgage lenders often hesitate to approve loans for properties with leases under 70 years
  • Potential buyers might be put off by the prospect of lease extension costs
  • The property’s value could be significantly lower than comparable homes with longer leases

Homeowners in this position might wish to explore options such as negotiating a lease extension before putting their property on the market. In some cases, it could be worth consulting a solicitor specialising in Scottish property law to understand all available options.

Whilst short leases are less common in Scotland’s property market, being aware of potential complications could help homeowners navigate the sales process more effectively. Those concerned about their lease length might consider seeking professional advice early to understand how it could affect their selling prospects.

item.title: Housing affordability crisis: It’s time to ditch the current affordable housing model
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rethinking Affordable Housing: Is the Current Model Fit for Purpose?

The UK continues to grapple with a deepening affordable housing crisis, with recent figures revealing a significant shortfall in delivery. Whilst 2024/25 saw 64,762 additional affordable homes completed – the highest number in a decade – this remains woefully below the estimated annual requirement of 180,000 properties. With starts on new affordable homes dropping to just 45,418 in the same period, and social rent properties accounting for less than a fifth of these, many are questioning whether the current delivery model remains viable.

The Challenges of Private Sector Reliance

The core issue lies in the heavy dependence on private developers to deliver affordable housing through Section 106 agreements. This approach has shown limitations, particularly during market downturns when developers struggle to maintain both affordable quotas and commercial viability. Even in favourable conditions, most schemes rarely exceed 30% affordable housing provision – a figure that falls short of London's requirements and future ambitions for Grey Belt developments and new towns.

Several factors contribute to this systemic under-delivery:

  • The inherent tension between commercial viability and political expectations
  • Market fluctuations disproportionately affecting affordable housing delivery
  • An over-reliance on cross-subsidy models that may no longer be fit for purpose

Exploring Alternative Solutions

With the government's manifesto commitment to dramatically increase social and affordable housebuilding yet to materialise, housing professionals might consider whether alternative approaches could break the deadlock. The scale of the challenge becomes clear when considering that to meet both the 300,000 annual homes target and affordable housing need, approximately 60% of all new properties would need to be affordable – a ratio the current system cannot realistically deliver.

Potential avenues for reform could include:

  • Greater direct public sector involvement in housing delivery
  • New funding models specifically designed for affordable housing projects
  • Revised planning policies that separate affordable housing delivery from market fluctuations
  • Innovative partnerships between local authorities, housing associations and builders

As the debate continues, what remains clear is that maintaining the status quo risks perpetuating the housing shortage. With demand far outstripping supply, fresh thinking may be required to ensure all Britons have access to quality, affordable homes.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rethinking Affordable Housing: Is the Current Model Fit for Purpose?

The UK continues to grapple with a deepening affordable housing crisis, with recent figures revealing a significant shortfall in delivery. Whilst 2024/25 saw 64,762 additional affordable homes completed – the highest number in a decade – this remains woefully below the estimated annual requirement of 180,000 properties. With starts on new affordable homes dropping to just 45,418 in the same period, and social rent properties accounting for less than a fifth of these, many are questioning whether the current delivery model remains viable.

The Challenges of Private Sector Reliance

The core issue lies in the heavy dependence on private developers to deliver affordable housing through Section 106 agreements. This approach has shown limitations, particularly during market downturns when developers struggle to maintain both affordable quotas and commercial viability. Even in favourable conditions, most schemes rarely exceed 30% affordable housing provision – a figure that falls short of London's requirements and future ambitions for Grey Belt developments and new towns.

Several factors contribute to this systemic under-delivery:

  • The inherent tension between commercial viability and political expectations
  • Market fluctuations disproportionately affecting affordable housing delivery
  • An over-reliance on cross-subsidy models that may no longer be fit for purpose

Exploring Alternative Solutions

With the government's manifesto commitment to dramatically increase social and affordable housebuilding yet to materialise, housing professionals might consider whether alternative approaches could break the deadlock. The scale of the challenge becomes clear when considering that to meet both the 300,000 annual homes target and affordable housing need, approximately 60% of all new properties would need to be affordable – a ratio the current system cannot realistically deliver.

Potential avenues for reform could include:

  • Greater direct public sector involvement in housing delivery
  • New funding models specifically designed for affordable housing projects
  • Revised planning policies that separate affordable housing delivery from market fluctuations
  • Innovative partnerships between local authorities, housing associations and builders

As the debate continues, what remains clear is that maintaining the status quo risks perpetuating the housing shortage. With demand far outstripping supply, fresh thinking may be required to ensure all Britons have access to quality, affordable homes.

item.title: Industry warning as vast majority of property firms face financial difficulties
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Construction Sector Faces Mounting Financial Pressures

New research reveals nearly 90% of UK property and construction firms are either currently experiencing financial distress or anticipate serious difficulties within the next eight months. The findings from Menzies LLP's 'Fixing the Foundations' report, based on surveys of 250 industry leaders, highlight growing challenges across the sector.

Key concerns include late payments affecting 93% of businesses, with invoices typically settled 53 days beyond agreed terms. This persistent cashflow issue forces many firms to effectively bankroll projects using their own working capital while awaiting payments - a practice that may increase financial vulnerability.

Inflation Squeezes Profit Margins

The report identifies multiple pressures eroding profitability:

  • 23% of respondents cite unsustainable strain from rising labour and material costs
  • 20% report pre-inflation contracts now delivering significantly lower profits
  • 18% find project delays have rendered some developments financially unviable

Industry experts warn these financial challenges could have wider implications for housing supply and infrastructure development if left unaddressed. With construction playing a vital role in UK economic growth, the sector's financial health remains crucial for meeting national housing targets.

As firms navigate these turbulent conditions, careful financial planning and robust cashflow management may prove essential for weathering current market challenges.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Construction Sector Faces Mounting Financial Pressures

New research reveals nearly 90% of UK property and construction firms are either currently experiencing financial distress or anticipate serious difficulties within the next eight months. The findings from Menzies LLP's 'Fixing the Foundations' report, based on surveys of 250 industry leaders, highlight growing challenges across the sector.

Key concerns include late payments affecting 93% of businesses, with invoices typically settled 53 days beyond agreed terms. This persistent cashflow issue forces many firms to effectively bankroll projects using their own working capital while awaiting payments - a practice that may increase financial vulnerability.

Inflation Squeezes Profit Margins

The report identifies multiple pressures eroding profitability:

  • 23% of respondents cite unsustainable strain from rising labour and material costs
  • 20% report pre-inflation contracts now delivering significantly lower profits
  • 18% find project delays have rendered some developments financially unviable

Industry experts warn these financial challenges could have wider implications for housing supply and infrastructure development if left unaddressed. With construction playing a vital role in UK economic growth, the sector's financial health remains crucial for meeting national housing targets.

As firms navigate these turbulent conditions, careful financial planning and robust cashflow management may prove essential for weathering current market challenges.

item.title: Methodology revealed behind Citizens Advice claim on letting agent rule-breaking
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Citizens Advice has revealed the methodology behind its recent claim that nearly half of private renters in England have experienced rule-breaking behaviour by letting agents over the past three years. The charity’s findings, which suggest that 48% of tenants have faced such issues, have sparked discussions about the rental sector’s practices. However, some property professionals have raised questions about how the research was conducted, prompting Citizens Advice to provide further details on its approach.

Understanding the Methodology

The research was based on a survey conducted by Yonder Data Solutions between 5 and 20 March 2026. A total of 4,017 private renters across England were polled, with separate sample groups focusing on different stages of the rental journey. These included tenants currently living in agent-managed properties, those who had recently moved through a letting agent, and those who had moved out of a property managed by an agent within the past three years.

Citizens Advice explained that its headline figure of 48% was derived from responses by tenants who had interacted with letting agents during their rental experience. This included those currently renting, as well as those who had moved into or out of a property managed by an agent in the previous three years. The charity emphasised that its findings reflect tenants’ reported experiences rather than independently verified incidents.

Defining Rule-Breaking Behaviour

The study’s definition of “rule-breaking behaviour” extended beyond formal regulatory breaches. It encompassed a wide range of tenant-reported issues, such as:

  • Not being provided with essential property information, such as an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC).
  • Receiving incorrect or misleading details about a property.
  • Feeling pressured into accepting a tenancy or opting for a zero-deposit scheme.
  • Agents failing to give appropriate notice before property visits.
  • Concerns about the suitability of tradespeople sent to carry out repairs.
  • Issues relating to the return of tenancy deposits.

These experiences, while not always constituting formal breaches, highlight areas where tenants feel let down or unfairly treated by letting agents.

Estimating the Scale of the Issue

To estimate that nearly four million renters nationwide may have encountered such behaviour, Citizens Advice combined its survey results with data from the English Housing Survey. This included information on the size of the private rented sector and average household composition. The charity’s approach aimed to provide a clearer picture of the scale of the issue, though it acknowledged the reliance on self-reported data.

While the findings have drawn attention to potential shortcomings in the rental sector, they also underscore the importance of transparency and accountability in letting agents’ practices. Tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with their rights and seek advice if they encounter issues during their rental journey.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Citizens Advice has revealed the methodology behind its recent claim that nearly half of private renters in England have experienced rule-breaking behaviour by letting agents over the past three years. The charity’s findings, which suggest that 48% of tenants have faced such issues, have sparked discussions about the rental sector’s practices. However, some property professionals have raised questions about how the research was conducted, prompting Citizens Advice to provide further details on its approach.

Understanding the Methodology

The research was based on a survey conducted by Yonder Data Solutions between 5 and 20 March 2026. A total of 4,017 private renters across England were polled, with separate sample groups focusing on different stages of the rental journey. These included tenants currently living in agent-managed properties, those who had recently moved through a letting agent, and those who had moved out of a property managed by an agent within the past three years.

Citizens Advice explained that its headline figure of 48% was derived from responses by tenants who had interacted with letting agents during their rental experience. This included those currently renting, as well as those who had moved into or out of a property managed by an agent in the previous three years. The charity emphasised that its findings reflect tenants’ reported experiences rather than independently verified incidents.

Defining Rule-Breaking Behaviour

The study’s definition of “rule-breaking behaviour” extended beyond formal regulatory breaches. It encompassed a wide range of tenant-reported issues, such as:

  • Not being provided with essential property information, such as an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC).
  • Receiving incorrect or misleading details about a property.
  • Feeling pressured into accepting a tenancy or opting for a zero-deposit scheme.
  • Agents failing to give appropriate notice before property visits.
  • Concerns about the suitability of tradespeople sent to carry out repairs.
  • Issues relating to the return of tenancy deposits.

These experiences, while not always constituting formal breaches, highlight areas where tenants feel let down or unfairly treated by letting agents.

Estimating the Scale of the Issue

To estimate that nearly four million renters nationwide may have encountered such behaviour, Citizens Advice combined its survey results with data from the English Housing Survey. This included information on the size of the private rented sector and average household composition. The charity’s approach aimed to provide a clearer picture of the scale of the issue, though it acknowledged the reliance on self-reported data.

While the findings have drawn attention to potential shortcomings in the rental sector, they also underscore the importance of transparency and accountability in letting agents’ practices. Tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with their rights and seek advice if they encounter issues during their rental journey.

item.title: Referral fees can benefit consumers – but transparency must improve, regulator says
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Referral fees between estate agents and conveyancers can offer advantages to consumers, but greater transparency is needed, according to recent findings from the Council for Licensed Conveyancers (CLC). The review, prompted by concerns raised in last year’s BBC Panorama investigation, highlights the potential benefits of such arrangements whilst calling for improved documentation and disclosure practices. Whilst referral fees remain a contentious issue in the property sector, the CLC has stopped short of recommending a ban, acknowledging their role in streamlining the home-buying process.

The Role of Referral Fees in Conveyancing

Referral fees are payments made by conveyancers to estate agents or other intermediaries for recommending their services. The CLC’s review suggests these arrangements can provide consumers with more convenient access to legal services and foster closer collaboration between estate agents and conveyancers. However, concerns persist about the potential for conflicts of interest, particularly when buyers feel pressured to use specific services as part of the home-buying process.

The review examined 12 licensed conveyancing practices of varying sizes, revealing that all operated referral arrangements. This underscores how prevalent the practice is within the industry. However, the reliance on referrals varies significantly. Three firms reported that 85% or more of their work came from referrals, whilst others obtained less than 15% of their instructions through such arrangements.

Transparency and Documentation Issues

Despite the potential benefits, the review identified significant shortcomings in how referral arrangements are documented and disclosed. Five of the firms reviewed were unable to provide written referral agreements when requested, and disclosures to clients were often incomplete or unavailable for inspection. This lack of transparency creates what the CLC describes as a regulatory “blind spot,” potentially leaving consumers unaware of the financial relationships behind their recommended services.

Referral fees themselves can vary widely, reportedly ranging from around £50 to over £1,000 per transaction. Critics argue that such payments can incentivise steering, where buyers are directed towards certain services regardless of whether they are the best fit. On the other hand, supporters contend that referral fees provide a legitimate marketing channel and can enhance the customer experience by creating a more integrated process.

Recommendations for Improvement

In response to its findings, the CLC has proposed several recommendations to strengthen transparency and accountability. These include ensuring that referral agreements are properly documented and that disclosures to clients are clear and readily available. The regulator also emphasises the need for firms to maintain robust records of all referral arrangements, enabling better oversight and compliance.

For homeowners navigating the property market, understanding the role of referral fees could help inform their decisions. Whilst these arrangements can offer convenience, borrowers might consider seeking independent advice to ensure they are receiving the most suitable services for their needs.

The CLC’s review highlights the dual nature of referral fees in the conveyancing market. Whilst they can streamline the home-buying process, improved transparency and documentation are essential to protect consumers and maintain trust in the sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Referral fees between estate agents and conveyancers can offer advantages to consumers, but greater transparency is needed, according to recent findings from the Council for Licensed Conveyancers (CLC). The review, prompted by concerns raised in last year’s BBC Panorama investigation, highlights the potential benefits of such arrangements whilst calling for improved documentation and disclosure practices. Whilst referral fees remain a contentious issue in the property sector, the CLC has stopped short of recommending a ban, acknowledging their role in streamlining the home-buying process.

The Role of Referral Fees in Conveyancing

Referral fees are payments made by conveyancers to estate agents or other intermediaries for recommending their services. The CLC’s review suggests these arrangements can provide consumers with more convenient access to legal services and foster closer collaboration between estate agents and conveyancers. However, concerns persist about the potential for conflicts of interest, particularly when buyers feel pressured to use specific services as part of the home-buying process.

The review examined 12 licensed conveyancing practices of varying sizes, revealing that all operated referral arrangements. This underscores how prevalent the practice is within the industry. However, the reliance on referrals varies significantly. Three firms reported that 85% or more of their work came from referrals, whilst others obtained less than 15% of their instructions through such arrangements.

Transparency and Documentation Issues

Despite the potential benefits, the review identified significant shortcomings in how referral arrangements are documented and disclosed. Five of the firms reviewed were unable to provide written referral agreements when requested, and disclosures to clients were often incomplete or unavailable for inspection. This lack of transparency creates what the CLC describes as a regulatory “blind spot,” potentially leaving consumers unaware of the financial relationships behind their recommended services.

Referral fees themselves can vary widely, reportedly ranging from around £50 to over £1,000 per transaction. Critics argue that such payments can incentivise steering, where buyers are directed towards certain services regardless of whether they are the best fit. On the other hand, supporters contend that referral fees provide a legitimate marketing channel and can enhance the customer experience by creating a more integrated process.

Recommendations for Improvement

In response to its findings, the CLC has proposed several recommendations to strengthen transparency and accountability. These include ensuring that referral agreements are properly documented and that disclosures to clients are clear and readily available. The regulator also emphasises the need for firms to maintain robust records of all referral arrangements, enabling better oversight and compliance.

For homeowners navigating the property market, understanding the role of referral fees could help inform their decisions. Whilst these arrangements can offer convenience, borrowers might consider seeking independent advice to ensure they are receiving the most suitable services for their needs.

The CLC’s review highlights the dual nature of referral fees in the conveyancing market. Whilst they can streamline the home-buying process, improved transparency and documentation are essential to protect consumers and maintain trust in the sector.

item.title: Landlords face growing compliance burden – here are the key dates
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlords face mounting compliance deadlines

Private landlords across England and Wales are being warned about an increasing number of regulatory deadlines, with failure to comply potentially resulting in substantial fines. The latest requirement under the Renters' Rights Act passed on 31 May, obliging landlords to provide existing tenants with specific documentation. Those who missed this deadline could face penalties of up to £7,000 per tenancy.

Property experts highlight that this is merely the first in a series of important dates landlords must track as new legislation comes into force. Jack Malnick of Landlord Resource notes that many property owners are finding it challenging to keep pace with the volume of legislative changes, raising the risk of inadvertent breaches.

Key dates for landlord diaries

Several critical deadlines are approaching that landlords might wish to note:

  • 31st July 2026 – Existing Section 21 and Section 8 notices must reach court by this date or become invalid
  • Late 2026 – Launch of the Private Rented Sector (PRS) database (exact date to be confirmed), requiring landlords to register themselves, their properties and compliance status regionally
  • Q4 2028 – Mandatory registration on the PRS Database becomes a legal requirement before any property can be let or advertised

Malnick cautions that the financial implications of non-compliance could be severe: "Beyond the initial £7,000 per tenancy penalty, persistent breaches may trigger additional civil penalties of up to £40,000. When combined with potential Rent Repayment Orders covering 24 months, total costs could surpass £80,000."

Navigating the changing landscape

The property sector is undergoing significant regulatory transformation, with many compliance failures occurring due to administrative oversights rather than deliberate neglect. Landlords might consider reviewing their processes and documentation to ensure they meet all current requirements.

With further changes on the horizon, including the introduction of a private rented sector ombudsman and mandatory database registration, staying informed about legislative developments could help landlords avoid costly penalties. Professional advice may be worthwhile for those managing larger portfolios or struggling with the new requirements.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlords face mounting compliance deadlines

Private landlords across England and Wales are being warned about an increasing number of regulatory deadlines, with failure to comply potentially resulting in substantial fines. The latest requirement under the Renters' Rights Act passed on 31 May, obliging landlords to provide existing tenants with specific documentation. Those who missed this deadline could face penalties of up to £7,000 per tenancy.

Property experts highlight that this is merely the first in a series of important dates landlords must track as new legislation comes into force. Jack Malnick of Landlord Resource notes that many property owners are finding it challenging to keep pace with the volume of legislative changes, raising the risk of inadvertent breaches.

Key dates for landlord diaries

Several critical deadlines are approaching that landlords might wish to note:

  • 31st July 2026 – Existing Section 21 and Section 8 notices must reach court by this date or become invalid
  • Late 2026 – Launch of the Private Rented Sector (PRS) database (exact date to be confirmed), requiring landlords to register themselves, their properties and compliance status regionally
  • Q4 2028 – Mandatory registration on the PRS Database becomes a legal requirement before any property can be let or advertised

Malnick cautions that the financial implications of non-compliance could be severe: "Beyond the initial £7,000 per tenancy penalty, persistent breaches may trigger additional civil penalties of up to £40,000. When combined with potential Rent Repayment Orders covering 24 months, total costs could surpass £80,000."

Navigating the changing landscape

The property sector is undergoing significant regulatory transformation, with many compliance failures occurring due to administrative oversights rather than deliberate neglect. Landlords might consider reviewing their processes and documentation to ensure they meet all current requirements.

With further changes on the horizon, including the introduction of a private rented sector ombudsman and mandatory database registration, staying informed about legislative developments could help landlords avoid costly penalties. Professional advice may be worthwhile for those managing larger portfolios or struggling with the new requirements.

item.title: Long-term renting becomes the norm as buyers remain locked out of housing market
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Long-Term Renting Now the Norm for Many UK Households

New research reveals that six in ten tenants across England and Wales are renting for significantly longer than they initially anticipated. The findings highlight how rising property prices and mortgage affordability challenges are preventing many households from making the transition to homeownership, turning what was once considered a temporary solution into a long-term housing arrangement.

The Changing Landscape of Renting

The study, based on responses from 650 landlords and tenants, found that 40% of renters have remained in the rental sector much longer than originally planned, with a further 20% stating they've rented somewhat longer than expected. Only 1% of respondents reported successfully moving into homeownership.

Tenancy durations also reflect this shift, with 59% of renters having lived in their current property for at least three years. Nearly a quarter (23%) have stayed in the same rental home for over a decade, while just 5% moved into their current accommodation within the past year.

Barriers to Homeownership

When asked about the reasons for prolonged renting, respondents cited familiar obstacles including:

  • Soaring property prices pushing homes out of reach
  • Stricter mortgage affordability tests
  • Difficulty saving for deposits while covering rising rental costs

Attitudes towards extended renting periods were mixed. Among those renting longer than expected, 40% said they had accepted the situation but still hoped to buy in future, while 23% appeared to have adjusted to the reality of long-term renting.

The research suggests the rental market is evolving to reflect these changes, with many tenants already adopting housing patterns that align with forthcoming Renters' Rights Act provisions. As the housing landscape continues to shift, these findings highlight the growing challenges facing aspiring homeowners across the country.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan
