Long-Term Renting Now the Norm for Many UK Households

New research reveals that six in ten tenants across England and Wales are renting for significantly longer than they initially anticipated. The findings highlight how rising property prices and mortgage affordability challenges are preventing many households from making the transition to homeownership, turning what was once considered a temporary solution into a long-term housing arrangement.

The Changing Landscape of Renting

The study, based on responses from 650 landlords and tenants, found that 40% of renters have remained in the rental sector much longer than originally planned, with a further 20% stating they've rented somewhat longer than expected. Only 1% of respondents reported successfully moving into homeownership.

Tenancy durations also reflect this shift, with 59% of renters having lived in their current property for at least three years. Nearly a quarter (23%) have stayed in the same rental home for over a decade, while just 5% moved into their current accommodation within the past year.

Barriers to Homeownership

When asked about the reasons for prolonged renting, respondents cited familiar obstacles including:

  • Soaring property prices pushing homes out of reach
  • Stricter mortgage affordability tests
  • Difficulty saving for deposits while covering rising rental costs

Attitudes towards extended renting periods were mixed. Among those renting longer than expected, 40% said they had accepted the situation but still hoped to buy in future, while 23% appeared to have adjusted to the reality of long-term renting.

The research suggests the rental market is evolving to reflect these changes, with many tenants already adopting housing patterns that align with forthcoming Renters' Rights Act provisions. As the housing landscape continues to shift, these findings highlight the growing challenges facing aspiring homeowners across the country.

item.title: Annual UK house price growth down to 1.7% for May
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices saw a modest annual increase of 1.7% in May, marking a slowdown in growth compared to previous months. This trend reflects the ongoing impact of higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty, which continue to influence buyer demand and market activity.

Factors Influencing the Slowdown

The dip in house price growth can be attributed to several key factors. Rising borrowing costs have made mortgages more expensive, reducing affordability for many prospective buyers. Additionally, geopolitical tensions and economic instability have created a cautious atmosphere, leading to a more subdued property market. These elements combined have contributed to the slower pace of annual growth observed in May.

Regional Variations in House Prices

Whilst the overall trend shows a deceleration, there are notable regional differences in house price performance. Some areas continue to experience stronger growth, driven by local demand and limited housing supply. Conversely, regions with higher property prices or weaker economic conditions have seen more pronounced slowdowns. Homeowners and buyers may wish to consider these variations when assessing the market landscape.

What This Means for Borrowers

For those looking to secure a mortgage or move home, the current market conditions present both challenges and opportunities. Whilst higher interest rates may impact affordability, the slower pace of price growth could offer more favourable conditions for negotiation. Borrowers might consider seeking advice from mortgage brokers to explore available options and ensure they secure competitive rates tailored to their financial circumstances.

As the UK property market navigates these complexities, staying informed about trends and developments remains crucial for homeowners and potential buyers alike.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices saw a modest annual increase of 1.7% in May, marking a slowdown in growth compared to previous months. This trend reflects the ongoing impact of higher mortgage rates and broader economic uncertainty, which continue to influence buyer demand and market activity.

Factors Influencing the Slowdown

The dip in house price growth can be attributed to several key factors. Rising borrowing costs have made mortgages more expensive, reducing affordability for many prospective buyers. Additionally, geopolitical tensions and economic instability have created a cautious atmosphere, leading to a more subdued property market. These elements combined have contributed to the slower pace of annual growth observed in May.

Regional Variations in House Prices

Whilst the overall trend shows a deceleration, there are notable regional differences in house price performance. Some areas continue to experience stronger growth, driven by local demand and limited housing supply. Conversely, regions with higher property prices or weaker economic conditions have seen more pronounced slowdowns. Homeowners and buyers may wish to consider these variations when assessing the market landscape.

What This Means for Borrowers

For those looking to secure a mortgage or move home, the current market conditions present both challenges and opportunities. Whilst higher interest rates may impact affordability, the slower pace of price growth could offer more favourable conditions for negotiation. Borrowers might consider seeking advice from mortgage brokers to explore available options and ensure they secure competitive rates tailored to their financial circumstances.

As the UK property market navigates these complexities, staying informed about trends and developments remains crucial for homeowners and potential buyers alike.

item.title: Only 5 Land Registry e-signatures in Q1
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Land Registry's push towards digital transformation appears to be progressing slower than anticipated, with new figures revealing just five qualified electronic signatures (QES) were used for property transactions in the first quarter of 2024. The statistics, obtained through a Freedom of Information request, highlight the ongoing challenges in modernising the conveyancing process.

Slow Adoption of Digital Signatures

Despite concerted efforts to digitise property transactions, the take-up of QES technology remains remarkably low. These advanced electronic signatures, which carry the same legal weight as handwritten ones under UK law, were introduced to streamline the home buying process. However, industry observers note that both conveyancers and homeowners continue to favour traditional methods.

Several factors may be contributing to this sluggish adoption:

  • Familiarity with established paper-based processes
  • Concerns about the technical requirements for QES
  • Limited awareness among smaller conveyancing firms
  • The need for face-to-face verification in some cases

Implications for the Property Market

The minimal use of QES suggests that the property sector's digital transformation may take longer than initially projected. Whilst the Land Registry has been actively promoting digital services through its Digital Street initiative, these latest figures indicate that behavioural change remains a significant hurdle.

Mortgage applicants and property buyers might find the slow pace of digitisation surprising, particularly given the widespread adoption of electronic signatures in other sectors. However, given the substantial financial stakes involved in property transactions, caution appears to be prevailing over convenience for the time being.

As the property market continues to evolve, industry watchers will be monitoring whether QES usage increases in subsequent quarters. For now, the figures serve as a reminder that even well-intentioned digital innovations can face substantial adoption challenges in traditional sectors like conveyancing.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Land Registry's push towards digital transformation appears to be progressing slower than anticipated, with new figures revealing just five qualified electronic signatures (QES) were used for property transactions in the first quarter of 2024. The statistics, obtained through a Freedom of Information request, highlight the ongoing challenges in modernising the conveyancing process.

Slow Adoption of Digital Signatures

Despite concerted efforts to digitise property transactions, the take-up of QES technology remains remarkably low. These advanced electronic signatures, which carry the same legal weight as handwritten ones under UK law, were introduced to streamline the home buying process. However, industry observers note that both conveyancers and homeowners continue to favour traditional methods.

Several factors may be contributing to this sluggish adoption:

  • Familiarity with established paper-based processes
  • Concerns about the technical requirements for QES
  • Limited awareness among smaller conveyancing firms
  • The need for face-to-face verification in some cases

Implications for the Property Market

The minimal use of QES suggests that the property sector's digital transformation may take longer than initially projected. Whilst the Land Registry has been actively promoting digital services through its Digital Street initiative, these latest figures indicate that behavioural change remains a significant hurdle.

Mortgage applicants and property buyers might find the slow pace of digitisation surprising, particularly given the widespread adoption of electronic signatures in other sectors. However, given the substantial financial stakes involved in property transactions, caution appears to be prevailing over convenience for the time being.

As the property market continues to evolve, industry watchers will be monitoring whether QES usage increases in subsequent quarters. For now, the figures serve as a reminder that even well-intentioned digital innovations can face substantial adoption challenges in traditional sectors like conveyancing.

item.title: £1.8bn of properties available to investors
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Commercial property investors currently have access to £1.8bn worth of mixed-use opportunities across England, with London and the North West emerging as particularly active markets. This substantial pool of available properties presents diverse options for those looking to expand their portfolios with assets combining residential, retail and office spaces.

Regional hotspots for mixed-use investments

The capital continues to dominate the commercial property landscape, accounting for a significant portion of available mixed-use listings. However, the North West has seen notable growth in recent months, with cities like Manchester and Liverpool offering competitive yields alongside strong tenant demand. Other regions showing promising activity include:

  • The West Midlands, particularly Birmingham's regeneration zones
  • Key South Coast locations with thriving tourism sectors
  • Selected urban centres in Yorkshire with transport infrastructure improvements

Factors driving investor interest

Several market conditions are making mixed-use properties increasingly attractive to commercial buyers. The diversification potential of these assets allows investors to spread risk across different property sectors within a single acquisition. Current trends influencing the market include:

  • Changing work patterns creating demand for flexible commercial spaces
  • Urban regeneration projects enhancing the value of well-located properties
  • Strong rental demand in city centres with good transport links

While the £1.8bn figure indicates substantial availability, prospective investors might wish to conduct thorough due diligence, particularly regarding local market conditions and future development plans in their target areas. Professional valuation and legal advice remain essential when considering any commercial property purchase.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Commercial property investors currently have access to £1.8bn worth of mixed-use opportunities across England, with London and the North West emerging as particularly active markets. This substantial pool of available properties presents diverse options for those looking to expand their portfolios with assets combining residential, retail and office spaces.

The capital continues to dominate the commercial property landscape, accounting for a significant portion of available mixed-use listings. However, the North West has seen notable growth in recent months, with cities like Manchester and Liverpool offering competitive yields alongside strong tenant demand. Other regions showing promising activity include:

  • The West Midlands, particularly Birmingham's regeneration zones
  • Key South Coast locations with thriving tourism sectors
  • Selected urban centres in Yorkshire with transport infrastructure improvements

Factors driving investor interest

Several market conditions are making mixed-use properties increasingly attractive to commercial buyers. The diversification potential of these assets allows investors to spread risk across different property sectors within a single acquisition. Current trends influencing the market include:

  • Changing work patterns creating demand for flexible commercial spaces
  • Urban regeneration projects enhancing the value of well-located properties
  • Strong rental demand in city centres with good transport links

While the £1.8bn figure indicates substantial availability, prospective investors might wish to conduct thorough due diligence, particularly regarding local market conditions and future development plans in their target areas. Professional valuation and legal advice remain essential when considering any commercial property purchase.
